Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Service_rifle "Special:EditPage/Service rifle") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Service rifle"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Service+rifle%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Service+rifle%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Service+rifle%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Service+rifle%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Service+rifle%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Service+rifle%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(March 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Service+rifle%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Service+rifle%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Service+rifle%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Service+rifle%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(March 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(or **standard-issue rifle**) is any [rifle](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle") a [military](/wiki/Military "Military") issues to its regular [soldiers](/wiki/Soldiers "Soldiers") or [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"). In modern militaries, this is generally a versatile, rugged, and reliable [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle") or [battle rifle](/wiki/Battle_rifle "Battle rifle"), suitable for use in nearly all environments and is effective in most [combat](/wiki/Combat "Combat") situations. Service rifles are solely designated and used as the primary weapon in almost all instances of [warfare](/wiki/Warfare "Warfare") and [training](/wiki/Military_training "Military training"). Almost all modern militaries are also issued [service pistols](/wiki/Service_pistol "Service pistol") as [sidearms](/wiki/Sidearm_(weapon) "Sidearm (weapon)") to accompany their service rifles. The term can also be used to describe weapons issued by non-military forces, such as [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement") or [paramilitaries](/wiki/Paramilitaries "Paramilitaries").
McNab, Chris (2013). *German Automatic Rifles 1941-45: Gew 41, Gew 43, FG 42 and StG 44*. Osprey Publishing. p. 19.
Grant, Niel (2015). *Mauser Military Rifles*. Osprey Publishing. p. 65.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Rifle issued to military personnel

"Infantry rifle" redirects here. For the British Baker rifle, officially known as the Infantry rifle, see [Baker rifle](/wiki/Baker_rifle "Baker rifle").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Service_rifle "Special:EditPage/Service rifle") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Service rifle"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Service+rifle%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Service+rifle%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Service+rifle%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Service+rifle%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Service+rifle%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Service+rifle%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(March 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Li%C3%A8ge%2C_Mus%C3%A9e_des_armes%2C_Grand_Curtius._FN_pistolet-mitrailleur_UZI%2C_ca_1960_%28002%29%2C_FN_fusil_automatique_l%C3%A9ger%2C_ca_1960_%28003%29%2C_FN_mitrailleuse_MINIMI_M%2C_1980-90_%28004%29.JPG/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Li%C3%A8ge,_Mus%C3%A9e_des_armes,_Grand_Curtius._FN_pistolet-mitrailleur_UZI,_ca_1960_(002),_FN_fusil_automatique_l%C3%A9ger,_ca_1960_(003),_FN_mitrailleuse_MINIMI_M,_1980-90_(004).JPG)

An [Uzi](/wiki/Uzi "Uzi") [submachine gun](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"), [FN FAL](/wiki/FN_FAL "FN FAL") [battle rifle](/wiki/Battle_rifle "Battle rifle"), and [FN Minimi](/wiki/FN_Minimi "FN Minimi") [light machine gun](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun")—common service weapons in the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") during the mid-to-late 20th century—displayed at the [Curtius Museum](/wiki/Curtius_Museum "Curtius Museum") in [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium")

A **service rifle** (or **standard-issue rifle**) is any [rifle](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle") a [military](/wiki/Military "Military") issues to its regular [soldiers](/wiki/Soldiers "Soldiers") or [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"). In modern militaries, this is generally a versatile, rugged, and reliable [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle") or [battle rifle](/wiki/Battle_rifle "Battle rifle"), suitable for use in nearly all environments and is effective in most [combat](/wiki/Combat "Combat") situations. Service rifles are solely designated and used as the primary weapon in almost all instances of [warfare](/wiki/Warfare "Warfare") and [training](/wiki/Military_training "Military training"). Almost all modern militaries are also issued [service pistols](/wiki/Service_pistol "Service pistol") as [sidearms](/wiki/Sidearm_(weapon) "Sidearm (weapon)") to accompany their service rifles. The term can also be used to describe weapons issued by non-military forces, such as [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement") or [paramilitaries](/wiki/Paramilitaries "Paramilitaries").

If the issued weapon is not a rifle or carbine, but instead a different type of firearm intended to serve in a specialized role such as a [combat shotgun](/wiki/Combat_shotgun "Combat shotgun"), [submachine gun](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"), or [light machine gun](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun"), it is called a **service firearm** or **service weapon**.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Service_rifle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of the firearm](/wiki/History_of_the_firearm "History of the firearm")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/CF_Infantryman_with_C7A2.jpg/250px-CF_Infantryman_with_C7A2.jpg)](/wiki/File:CF_Infantryman_with_C7A2.jpg)

A [Canadian Army](/wiki/Canadian_Army "Canadian Army") soldier with his [Colt Canada C7A2](/wiki/Colt_Canada_C7 "Colt Canada C7") service rifle

Firearms with [rifled barrels](/wiki/Rifled_barrel "Rifled barrel") existed long before the 19th century, but were not widely used until the mid-19th century in conflicts such as the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") and [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). Thus, rifles in the early 19th century were for specialist marksmen only, whilst ordinary infantry were issued less accurate [smoothbore](/wiki/Smoothbore "Smoothbore") [muskets](/wiki/Musket "Musket") which had a higher rate of fire, with bore diameters as high as 19 mm (0.75 inch). Early "service rifles" of the 1840s and 1850s, such as the Swiss [Infanteriegewehr Modell 1842](/wiki/Infanteriegewehr_Modell_1842 "Infanteriegewehr Modell 1842"), the British [Pattern 1853 Enfield](/wiki/Pattern_1853_Enfield "Pattern 1853 Enfield"), and the American [Springfield Model 1840](/wiki/Springfield_Model_1840_flintlock_musket "Springfield Model 1840 flintlock musket") and [Springfield Model 1855](/wiki/Springfield_Model_1855 "Springfield Model 1855") were all muzzleloading muskets.

Ordnance rifles were introduced in the 1860s and 1870s, with the French [Chassepot Model 1866](/wiki/Chassepot "Chassepot"), the Swiss [Peabody *Gewehr Modell* 1867](/wiki/Peabody_action "Peabody action"), and the Prussian [Mauser Model 1871](/wiki/Mauser_Model_1871 "Mauser Model 1871"). In the United States, the [Springfield Model 1873](/wiki/Springfield_Model_1873 "Springfield Model 1873") was the first [single-shot](/wiki/Single-shot "Single-shot") breech-loading rifle adopted by the United States [War Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_War "United States Department of War") for manufacture and widespread issue to U.S. troops.

The development of *[Poudre B](/wiki/Poudre_B "Poudre B")* [smokeless powder](/wiki/Smokeless_powder "Smokeless powder") in 1884, introduced with the French [Lebel Model 1886 rifle](/wiki/Lebel_Model_1886_rifle "Lebel Model 1886 rifle"), spelt the end of [gunpowder warfare](/wiki/Gunpowder_warfare "Gunpowder warfare") and led to a jump in small arms development. By the beginning of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), all of the world's major powers had adopted repeating [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") rifles, such as the British [Lee–Enfield](/wiki/Lee%E2%80%93Enfield "Lee–Enfield"), the German [Gewehr 98](/wiki/Gewehr_98 "Gewehr 98"), and the Russian [Mosin–Nagant](/wiki/Mosin%E2%80%93Nagant "Mosin–Nagant").

During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the United States adopted the [M1 Garand](/wiki/M1_Garand "M1 Garand"), which was the first widely adopted semi-automatic rifle that was brought into military service in 1936. Despite advancements in rifle technology, the United States was the only country to adopt a [semi-automatic rifle](/wiki/Semi-automatic_rifle "Semi-automatic rifle") as their primary service rifle. While other countries did develop semi-automatic rifles later on and used in limited numbers during the war. For comparison, Germany produced 402,000 [Gewehr 43](/wiki/Gewehr_43 "Gewehr 43") rifles, compared to 14,000,000 of the [Karabiner 98k](/wiki/Karabiner_98k "Karabiner 98k") (a shortened variant of the Gewehr 98).[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) However, it was during the war that Germany also produced the [StG 44](/wiki/StG_44 "StG 44"), which is capable of controllable fully automatic fire from a 30-round magazine with the newly developed [7.92x33mm Kurz](/wiki/7.92x33mm_Kurz "7.92x33mm Kurz") intermediate rifle cartridge. After the war, the StG 44 was of particular interest to the Soviet Union, whose [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47") was derived heavily from the German design.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In the 1960s and 1970s, the United States developed and produced the [M16 rifle](/wiki/M16_rifle "M16 rifle"), cementing the applicability of the [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle") as an effective and versatile combat weapon for future conflicts.
